g.data-narration: Welcome to understanding how processors store data from registers into memory locations using MAR, MDR, and control signals.
A Store operation transfers data from a CPU register to a specified memory location, the reverse of a fetch operation.
We use register transfer notation: MAR holds the address, MDR holds the data, and memory writes at the specified location.
g.text: : Store contents of R1 into memory location LOC
: Place data to be stored in MDR
: Write MDR contents to memory at MAR address

g.data-narration: The store operation completes in five sequential steps: load address, load data, issue write, memory stores, and MFC signal.
Step one: The processor places the target memory address into the Memory Address Register (MAR) from the effective address.
g.text: from the instruction itself or be

g.data-narration: Step two: The processor copies the data to be stored from the source register into the Memory Data Register (MDR).
g.text: Data to be written is placed

g.data-narration: Step three: The processor activates the Write control signal on the control bus, signaling a write operation request.
Step four: Memory reads the address from MAR via address bus and data from MDR via data bus, then writes the data.
Step five: After writing, memory sends the MFC signal back to the processor, confirming the operation is complete.
The store operation typically requires three clock cycles: address/data load, write signal and bus transfer, then memory write and MFC.
g.text: Issue Write signal; data on buses

g.data-narration: Aligned stores access addresses that are multiples of word size and complete in one operation; unaligned stores may need two writes.
g.text: Address NOT multiple of word size
May require TWO memory write operations

g.data-narration: Big-endian stores the MSB at the lowest address; little-endian stores the LSB at the lowest address when storing multi-byte words.
Store operations execute in the MEM stage of the instruction pipeline: effective address calculated in EX, then data stored in MEM stage.
Six key points: Store transfers register to memory using MAR and MDR, issues Write signal, waits for MFC, and involves alignment and endianness.
g.text: Store transfers data from CPU register to memory: [LOC] ← R
MAR holds target address; MDR holds data to be written
Processor issues Write control signal and waits for MFC confirmation
Word alignment affects performance: aligned stores are preferred

g.data-narration: Endianness determines byte ordering in multi-byte stores. Store operations occur in MEM stage and are essential for systems programming.
g.text: Endianness determines byte ordering in multi-byte word storage
Store occurs in MEM stage of instruction cycle, no WB stage used
Applications: Systems programming, compiler optimization, embedded systems
